data.title: Kremlin warns Ukraine running out of time to accept peace terms as winter crisis deepens
data.description:
The Kremlin said on Thursday that Ukraine was running out of options to strike a deal to end almost four years of war with Russia, as thousands were without heating in Kyiv in -12 degrees Celsius (10 degrees Fahrenheit) temperatures following waves of Russian strikes on the country’s energy grid.
Moscow has not backed down from its intention to seize the rest of east Ukraine as the fourth anniversary of its offensive – Europe’s worst war since World War II – looms.
“The situation is deteriorating day by day for the Kyiv regime,” Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told reporters, adding that Ukraine’s “corridor for decision-making” was “narrowing”.
Russia has rejected Western proposals to end the war and said it is intent on taking the rest of Ukrainian land it has proclaimed as Russian by force if diplomacy fails.
“The time has come for [Ukrainian leader Volodymyr] Zelensky to take responsibility and make the appropriate decision,” Peskov said, apparently urging Kyiv to agree to Moscow’s hardline terms.
Russian President Vladimir Putin has demanded Kyiv withdraw from swathes of eastern and southern Ukraine as a precondition to a ceasefire.

US President Donald Trump has expressed frustration with both sides, with no breakthrough to end the war reached after months of talks.
Ukrainian, US and European negotiators have agreed a framework 20-point plan to end the war, Zelensky said – an adjustment to a 28-point proposal initially tabled by Washington that heavily favoured Moscow.
The Kremlin has balked at any adjustments.
Peskov said that dialogue was continuing with the United States.

The Europeans had many talks with the Americans, he said, and that “it is important that the Russian side also present its opinions on these discussions”.
Moscow had rejected a declaration that Britain and France would send troops to Ukraine after a ceasefire with Russia, calling them potential “legitimate targets” for Moscow.
February 24 will mark four years since the Kremlin launched its full-scale offensive on Ukraine.
Tens of thousands have been killed, much of eastern Ukraine has been decimated and millions have been forced to flee their homes.

data.title: China’s subsidy change adds to EV makers’ stress as more buyers consider petrol cars
data.description:
Growth momentum in the mainland car market may shift from battery-powered to petrol-based models this year on account of a revised subsidy policy, exacerbating a bearish profit outlook for the country’s 50-odd electric vehicle (EV) builders.
Slower growth in EV adoption would deal another heavy blow to assemblers from BYD to Leapmotor, which are known for low-priced cars, after big-name international banks reduced forecasts for car deliveries this year.
“Petroleum-powered cars will regain market share this year,” said Zhao Zhen, a sales director at Shanghai dealer Wan Zhuo Auto. “EV brands are under pressure to spur sales.”
According to data from the China Passenger Car Association (CPCA), 117,000 EVs, comprising pure electric and plug-in hybrid vehicles, were sold across mainland China in the first 11 days of the year, accounting for 35.7 per cent of total national car sales.
The percentage stands in stark contrast with an EV penetration ratio of 54 per cent across 2025.
Under Beijing’s new EV subsidy programme, buyers of an EV for replacement purposes get a subsidy equivalent to 12 per cent of the newly bought car’s price from January 1, which is capped at 20,000 yuan (US$2,868). The policy was put in place by the National Development and Reform Commission and the Ministry of Finance.
Those who purchase a petrol-powered vehicle get a 10 per cent subsidy, with the maximum amount set at 15,000 yuan.
In the previous two years, consumers stood to get a subsidy of 20,000 yuan for an EV, while receiving 15,000 yuan for buying a new petrol car after selling or disposing of their used car.

A buyer of an electric car priced at 100,000 yuan would receive a 12,000 yuan subsidy now – 8,000 yuan or 40 per cent less than last year.
Most budget-conscious consumers, facing a big decline in subsidy value, have either abandoned their purchase plans or chosen to adopt a wait-and-see attitude, according to dealers.
Low-priced electric cars in the sub-100,000 yuan segment have been a key growth driver for the Chinese car market since 2023 because the attractive subsidies, especially relative to petrol cars, lured thousands of young and low-income consumers.
A pure electric car can save a driver up to 10,000 yuan a year in fuel costs compared with a vehicle powered by an internal combustion engine.
“It is likely that some carmakers will offer discounts as compensation to lower subsidies,” said Chen Zihang, a young worker in Shanghai who looks to own a car this year. “We can wait until the total cost for owning an entry-level car is brought down.”
EV penetration in China, the world’s largest automotive and electric car market, grew by leaps and bounds over the past five years, jumping from about 5 per cent in 2020 to more than 50 per cent in 2025, CPCA data showed.
Mainland buyers had been exempt from a 10 per cent vehicle purchase tax on eligible green-car purchases through December 31 as part of Beijing’s push to curb emissions. From January 1, the tax was set at 5 per cent through the end of 2027, before returning to 10 per cent in 2028.
Deutsche Bank estimated mainland passenger-car sales would fall 5 per cent in 2026, while JPMorgan forecast deliveries by Chinese automotive groups could decline 3 to 5 per cent this year. Swiss Bank UBS predicted total deliveries could drop by 2 per cent this year.
It would be the first sales contraction on China’s auto market since 2020.
To date, fewer than five Chinese EV makers have turned profitable.
“A fast increase in EV penetration will not be seen this year,” said Paul Gong, head of China automotive research at UBS. “But we believe more consumers will choose electric cars. The full-year penetration number will still rise, though at a slower pace.”

data.title: Hong Kong police shoot man wielding meat cleaver in Tuen Mun
data.description:
Hong Kong police shot and injured a man after he brandished a meat cleaver in Tuen Mun neighbourhood.
The incident occurred at around 7pm along Tuen Hi Road near Tuen Mun Town Hall, where the man was seen carrying the knife.
An officer responding to the scene opened fire, with two shots believed to have been fired near a convenience store at Tuen Mun Town Plaza, according to a source.

A police spokesman said the man was unconscious when he was taken to Tuen Mun Hospital.
A video circulating online, apparently taken by a passer-by, showed three officers, one wearing a helmet, attending to an unresponsive person on the ground.
In the footage, another officer can be heard asking who fired the shots, as the helmeted officer raises his hand.
More to follow …

data.title: An internally split Europe can never fully engage China and Asia
data.description:
When contemporary Europe engages with the world, it increasingly presents as two distinct Europes operating within the same institutional framework. This duality – a Europe of strategic dependence vs a Europe of normative assertion – creates a contradiction.
For partners, particularly in China and across Asia, this is not merely an abstract identity crisis but a practical geopolitical puzzle that complicates engagement and challenges assumptions about Europe’s global role.
One Europe, embedded within Nato and the US-led transatlantic security architecture, anchors its survival to American military power and strategic priorities. This “Nato Europe” has internalised a position of security dependence, solidified through decades of integrated defence planning, intelligence sharing and military procurement tied to Washington. Its strategic subordination means its positions on critical issues – from technological competition, trade and tariff issues, and sanctions policy to the Taiwan question – are often structurally pre-aligned with US objectives.
The other “sociopolitical” Europe sees itself as a post-militarist, normative power. It projects influence through regulatory standards, values-based diplomacy and regulated capitalism, positioning itself as a moral and civilisational counterpoint to American unilateralism and other global models.
From the vantage point of China and Asia, Europe’s internal split translates into tangible challenges for cooperation. First, it creates a credibility deficit. When “sociopolitical Europe” advocates for a rules-based international order, champions multilateralism or criticises other powers’ internal affairs, its moral authority is undercut by the reality of “Nato Europe”.


The credibility of advocating for strategic autonomy or principled engagement is significantly undermined when final security sovereignty is entrusted to the United States, whose strategic objectives, especially towards China, are fundamentally confrontational.
For China and Asia, this renders European normative rhetoric selective and instrumental, seen less as a universal principle and more as an extension of a US-centric geopolitical framework. Europe’s tendency to lecture the world on values while outsourcing its security erodes its stature as an independent pole.
Second, this duality frustrates strategic and economic engagement with China. China-Europe relations are pulled in opposing directions. On one hand, the economic logic of deep interdependence and normative Europe’s interest in global governance encourage partnership. On the other, the security logic of “Nato Europe” injects suspicion and imposes alignment on containment policies.
This results in inconsistent, fragmented policies, where economic cooperation in green technology or infrastructure is undermined by political pressure on supply chain “de-risking” or hi-tech sanctions. For China, it raises a critical question: is it dealing with a sovereign Europe pursuing its interests, or a subordinate actor executing a strategy scripted elsewhere?
Third, for Asia and the Global South, Europe’s predicament highlights the risks of over-reliance on an external security guarantor. Many Asian nations carefully navigate between major powers, valuing strategic autonomy and refusing to choose sides. Europe serves as a cautionary example of how deep security integration can erode policy independence and constrain the ability to shape one’s destiny.


Furthermore, as Europe redirects resources to meet US demands for higher defence spending, its capacity for developmental investment, infrastructure partnerships and economic engagement in Asia – key pillars of its promised Indo-Pacific strategy – weakens. Europe risks becoming a less relevant economic partner for Asia, even as it seeks a greater geopolitical role.
Sociopolitical Europe’s self-image as a “third force” is, from an Asian and Chinese perspective, an illusion. It is, in practice, a normative-economic power operating within a security and geopolitical order defined and guaranteed by the US.
Its regulatory power, exemplified by the “Brussels effect”, is real but exists in a separate domain from hard security. In an era of intensifying great-power competition, this separation is unsustainable; normative influence untethered from strategic autonomy lacks ultimate weight.
Therefore, China and Asian partners increasingly adopt a pragmatic, composite approach to Europe. Engagement is becoming issue-specific and segmented. Cooperation may proceed robustly on climate change, digital governance or multilateral trade issues where normative Europe has agency.


However, on matters touching core US strategic interests – security architecture, military alignment or technology containment – engagement is cautious, anticipating the overwhelming pull of “Nato Europe”. This compartmentalisation reflects a rational adaptation.
For example, Europe responded with caution to the recent US military action in Venezuela, opting for “diplomatic” statements over explicit condemnation, while simultaneously responding more assertively towards the US talk of “annexing” Greenland for security.
The gap between Europe’s normative emphasis on Ukraine’s sovereignty and its reluctance to challenge a dominant ally highlights the divide between Europe’s security-dependent and norms-assertive faces. This duality undermines Europe’s credibility and strategic autonomy, especially in the eyes of China and Asia, who see inconsistent application of principles when great power interests are at stake.

Europe’s future as a global actor hinges on its ability to reconcile its two selves. It must choose between remaining a strategically dependent entity whose normative voice rings hollow or undertaking the arduous journey towards genuine strategic sovereignty that would give weight to its civilisational ambitions.
For China and Asia, this means managing relations with Europe with clear-eyed pragmatism. Europe will not be treated as a monolithic third force but as a complex, sometimes contradictory, composite actor.
The goal is not to push Europe to choose sides but to engage constructively where interests align, while hedging against the constraints imposed by its transatlantic security bondage. The responsibility for resolving its dilemma lies in Europe. Until it does, its partners in the East and Global South will continue to navigate the two Europes with calibrated and realistic expectations.

data.title: FMT apologises for racial question linking Malaysia’s ‘Chinese Problem’ and Palestine
data.description:
Free Malaysia Today (FMT) has issued an apology after one of its reporters posed a question during a public lecture that was believed to have carried racial overtones.
In a statement on Wednesday, FMT said that the incident occurred during a public lecture featuring United Kingdom politician George Galloway titled “Gaza Exposes the Complicity of International Actors” held on January 12.
“FMT wishes to put on record that it had no prior knowledge of the journalist’s intention to raise the question, nor did we approve of his comments.
“When the matter then became the subject of discussion on social media on January 13, FMT took immediate action by opening an internal investigation, which has led to disciplinary action being taken against the journalist.
“In the course of the investigation, it also came to FMT’s attention that, contrary to statements made by various persons on social media, the journalist in question did not use the word ‘apartheid’ at the forum. Be that as it may, FMT recognises the inherent sensitivities contained in the journalist’s comments and in the question, which he posed, which Mr Galloway rightly denounced,” the statement said.
The statement was signed by both FMT executive chairman Nelson Fernandez and managing director Azeem Abu Bakar.
FMT added that it regretted the “distress our journalist has caused the forum’s speaker, its organisers, the attendees and Malaysians at large”, and that it sincerely apologises for the issue.
A video of the question has been posted on social media, where the journalist asked if the speaker saw the parallels between “the Palestine problem and the Chinese problem in Malaysia”.
The journalist also asked if pro-Palestinian supporters vehemently rejected right-wing views, such as Zionism, why would they support right-wing ideals, such as anti-immigrant views.
This story was first published by The Star

data.title: Uber’s WeChat tie-up pays off as demand from Chinese tourists rises overseas
data.description:
Uber Technologies’ strategic partnership with Tencent Holdings’ super app WeChat is yielding significant dividends for the ride-hailing operator as it meets a critical demand for Chinese travellers overseas.
Since Uber launched a mini-program on WeChat in July, travellers from the mainland have been actively using Uber’s services via the new channel in 20 markets, said Anthony Wong, the head of business development at Uber in Asia-Pacific, on Thursday.
While the ride-hailing service provider operates in 70 markets globally, these 20 markets, which included the US, Europe, Japan and South Korea, were among the most important for the company, Wong said at a WeChat event in Guangzhou, the capital of Guangdong province. About 90 per cent of the mini-program users were new to Uber, he added.
In 2016, New York-listed Uber merged its mainland Chinese operations with Didi Chuxing, the mainland’s largest car-hailing platform, after years of a debilitating price war.

The budding partnership with WeChat, which has 1.4 billion monthly active users globally, has encouraged Uber to launch a new campaign to attract more Chinese users.
During the Lunar New Year in February, when mainland residents will enjoy a record nine-day holiday, Uber would offer a discount of at least 40 per cent for new users who join via the mini-program, Wong said.
Uber is a prime example of how Tencent continues to expand its ubiquitous mini-program ecosystem in China and overseas.
On Thursday, Tencent said merchants from more than 100 markets globally had launched mini-programs on WeChat that serve Chinese tourists as well as local residents. Some of the markets with 1 million monthly active users last year included Singapore, Japan and Hong Kong-Macau combined.
Some markets, such as Italy, witnessed exponential growth, with monthly transaction value there more than quadrupling in the second half of last year.
Tencent is seeking to expand its reach beyond just Chinese tourists overseas. It recently launched the “Tenpay Global Checkout” for mini-programs, which supports multiple payment methods, including digital wallets, local payment networks, and credit and debit cards, allowing users overseas to make payments using local payment methods they are familiar with.
WeChat, known as Weixin on the mainland, was also testing a new feature to redirect Google search results to mini-programs on the app, said Du Zhiyang, a representative from the WeChat mini-program team.
WeChat has formed other major partnerships to serve Chinese tourists. In April 2025, it introduced the Chinese-language mini-program for Tabelog, a popular Japanese restaurant review website, enabling mainland tourists to check restaurant locations, book tables and pay their bills in yuan.
A month earlier, Singapore rolled out a “Sentosa Discovery Guide” mini-program on WeChat, featuring a Mandarin interface.

data.title: Stalled work to resume as Hong Kong distributes 3,000 fire-tested scaffold nets
data.description:
Hong Kong’s construction industry body has begun distributing 3,000 rigorously tested scaffolding nets from a 12,000-unit shipment to 11 contractors overseeing 19 renovation sites, following the suspension of hundreds of projects after the deadly Tai Po inferno.
The Construction Industry Council (CIC) said on Thursday that it had received 12,000 centrally procured nets from Guangdong province, with the remaining 38,000 from the 50,000-unit order expected to arrive in phases by early February.
In total, the nets will supply 89 affected contractors managing 418 building projects across Hong Kong.
Executive director Albert Cheng Ting-ning said contractors could resume their stalled projects once they collected their nets at the council’s warehouse in San Tin.
“We are prioritising distribution according to the scheduled completion dates to facilitate the timely resumption of work,” Cheng said.
The new nets were produced by two qualified manufacturers in Jiangmen and Foshan, where they underwent initial flame-retardant testing. Each unit must pass a strict two-tier verification process before being deployed.
“To ensure total accountability, every net in this procurement is equipped with an RFID tracking system,” Cheng noted, referring to the radio frequency identification technology that uses radio waves to read data from small electronic tags.
“By scanning the QR code with a smartphone, stakeholders can instantly verify the manufacturer, production date and specific test results for the unit assigned to their site,” he added.
The government ordered the removal of all netting on 418 buildings undergoing renovations across the city last December, citing fire hazards after the devastating blaze at Wang Fuk Court claimed 168 lives.
The use of substandard, non-fire-retardant nets was suspected to have contributed to the rapid spread of the blaze.
The nets procured by the council cost around HK$170 (US$21.8) per unit – higher than standard flame-retardant nets which are priced at about HK$100.

“In order to guarantee a high standard of fire safety, we have introduced several additional stages of testing,” Cheng said. “While these testing fees make our nets more expensive than before, I believe this is a reasonable cost, as it ensures the nets meet safety standards and helps safeguard lives and property.”
Cheng noted that the centralised procurement was a transitional arrangement and that contractors would be required to source their own compliant materials in the future.
“Our goal is to provide a clear safety playbook for contractors to follow moving forward,” he said.
Andy Li Chi-kong, an executive at Build Craft Construction and Engineering, the company in charge of a residential project on Guildford Road on The Peak, said his firm procured 110 nets from this batch.
He said the price remained manageable despite the higher cost, noting that contractors had previously faced challenges sourcing genuine compliant materials amid the prevalence of substandard or counterfeit products in the market.
“Searching for individual manufacturers on our own will be time-consuming and labour intensive. I hope the council will consider continuing this centralised procurement in the long run,” he said.
Chau Sze-kit, chairman of the Hong Kong Construction Industry Employees General Union, noted that while there were 49 state-certified manufacturers on the mainland, many were initially reluctant to produce for the Hong Kong market due to the high-profile nature of the Tai Po fire and unclear technical specifications.
“The council’s current initiative has served as a critical demonstration, clarifying the rules of the game for these suppliers. I believe a lot of them will now start meeting demand from Hong Kong,” Chow said.
However, he said the supply chain faced a looming deadline with Lunar New Year approaching in mid-February.
“With mainland factories set to begin their holiday break in late January, sourcing will be extremely difficult in the weeks ahead. While the situation is expected to stabilise by early March as more manufacturers enter the market, the industry expects to face potential delays throughout the first half of 2026,” he said.

data.title: Nie Weiping, Chinese Go master known for dramatic victory over Japanese rival, dies at 73
data.description:
Nie Weiping, a legendary Go master in China, died in Beijing at the age of 73 on Wednesday.
Nie is best remembered for his dramatic victory in the first Japan-China Super Go in 1985. Few had hope that China would win because its players had long lagged behind Japanese competitors in the strategy board game.
By the time it was Nie’s turn to play in Tokyo, Japan’s top player, Kobayashi Koichi, had already won six straight games against his Chinese counterparts. Nie was the only Chinese player left to face Japan’s three highest ranked players.
Nie became a national hero after he defeated all three rivals and seized the championship after winning the final in Beijing. That same night, the Chinese women’s volleyball team defeated the Japanese team and won the FIVB Volleyball Women’s World Cup in Japan.
The victories sparked spontaneous celebrations in Beijing’s Tiananmen Square as euphoric crowds took to the streets. The victories were seen as an opportunity to restore national pride as China was emerging from the aftermath of the Cultural Revolution, seeking its own identity and starting to open up to the world.

An article published by the state-owned Beijing Daily in 2008 described the mixed feelings when China co-hosted the first Japan-China Super Go from 1984 to 1985.
“It was during the beginning of the economic reform and opening up and there was an influx of Japanese goods. The Chinese had mixed feelings towards Japan. On one hand, they envied the Japanese goods. On the other hand, they could not forget the old hostility from decades ago [during World War II],” it said.
“When China first opened its door [to the world], China badly needed a victory to prove itself, and this kind of sentiment was particularly strong when it was facing Japan.”
Nie’s victory ended Japan’s dominance in Go and made him a national icon – a status he had not previously enjoyed despite already ranking as a top player and winning his first national championship in 1975 and several more in the years that followed.
Nie had 11 successive wins in the Japan-China Super Go competitions from 1985 to 1987 and helped the Chinese team grab the championship for three consecutive years, winning him the nickname “Iron Gatekeeper”.
He became the head coach of the Chinese national Go team in 1986 and was awarded the title “Go Sage” by China’s national sports governing body in 1988. Nobody else has been granted the title in China.
Nie sparked a nationwide Go craze, with students forming clubs and associations for the game in universities across China.
“At that time, Chinese university students would be ashamed if they did not play Go,” according to the 2008 Beijing Daily article.

Born on August 17, 1952, in Shenzhou, Hebei province, Nie began learning the game at the age of nine and won the Beijing Children’s Championship a year later.
In his early years, he was friends with a young Xi Jinping, who would go on to become the Chinese president.
Nie told South Korean newspaper JoongAng Ilbo in 2015 that the two went to the same secondary school in Beijing.
Since Nie’s father was an intellectual and Xi’s father was a Communist Party elder, both families were purged during the Cultural Revolution and the two often spent time together, along with another friend from a similar background.
“When President Xi was working in Hebei province [as a county leader in the early 1980s], he would come to Beijing every weekend, and we would watch football matches together,” Nie was quoted as saying.
Nie told the South Korean newspaper that he would visit Xi as the latter moved from post to post for his career, though he said he eventually stopped visiting.
Nie is also known for his relationships with former paramount leader Deng Xiaoping and martial arts novelist Louis Cha Leung-yung, who went by the pen name Jin Yong. From 1983 to 1993, Nie often played bridge with Deng. He later praised the former leader for his sharp intellect, saying Deng often won.
Cha was eager to learn Go, and the writer called Nie his teacher starting in the 1980s.
Nie was sent to northeast China’s Heilongjiang province for six years of manual labour during the Cultural Revolution. Despite his talent as a child, he did not start his career as a Go player until 1973 when China rebuilt its national Go team.
Nie said he was drawn to Go and other mental games, rather than physical games, because he had a congenital heart condition. He was often seen with oxygen tanks and a breathing apparatus nearby during important matches. He was also known to smoke heavily during the matches.
The 1980s were his golden era as a player. His first major setback came when he was defeated by South Korea’s Cho Hun-hyun in a close match in 1989. The match was pivotal as it foreshadowed the rise of South Korean players in a game that had been dominated by the Chinese and Japanese.
Nie finished as a runner-up in world tournaments after that, including the 1990 Fujitsu Cup and the 1995 Dongyang Securities Cup.
While Nie never secured another international or Asian championship title after 1992, his legacy continued. He set up Nie Weiping Go Academy in 1999 and trained thousands of players. He was also a mentor to Chinese Go stars such as Chang Hao, Gu Li and Ke Jie.
He wrote a book, Nie Weiping on Go: The Art of Positional Judgment, which has been translated into English.
In 2017, Nie served as a commentator when Ke, China’s top player, competed against AlphaGo, an artificial intelligence (AI) program developed by Google. While Ke and Nie were both defeated by AlphaGo, Nie said the high-profile games against AI helped promote Go. He also said AI could help train human players.
Nie was diagnosed with rectal cancer in 2013 and had a stroke last year. His daughter said he died of a sudden illness on Wednesday.
Nie was married three times. He is survived by his wife and three children.

data.title: China targets wealthy influencers with audits, fines as fiscal pressures rise
data.description:
China has ramped up audits involving online influencers and high-wage earners, as part of broader efforts to expand tax revenue and refine the taxation system.
The State Taxation Administration on Wednesday spotlighted two fresh cases involving influencers in the centrally administered city of Chongqing and in Gansu province.
In Chongqing, authorities ordered an influencer surnamed Peng – who has 30 million followers and works in online video production and commercial advertising – to pay 4.15 million yuan (US$595,000) in back taxes and fines for under-reporting income.
In the other case, Gansu regulators ordered a live-streamer surnamed Yang, an e-commerce host with nearly 300,000 followers, to pay 1.81 million yuan for similar offences.
As China’s digital infrastructure matures, top-tier influencers are leveraging massive audience engagement to generate premium earnings. Consequently, they have become a focal point of Beijing’s intensified tax scrutiny as regulators seek to broaden the national tax base amid fiscal pressures.
“The scale and fluctuations in the declared income did not align with the influencers’ consistently high online traffic, raising suspicions that income had been concealed to evade taxes,” state tax officials said in a separate statement on Wednesday, explaining how an analysis of big data flagged the discrepancies.
The fresh disclosures follow a report by the taxation administration in December noting that it had investigated 1,818 “high-income, high-net-worth individuals” – including online influencers and celebrities – in the first 11 months of 2025, recovering 1.52 billion yuan in unpaid taxes.
The Chinese government has been grappling with fiscal pressures in recent years as a slowing economy and deflationary trends have reduced corporate profits, leading to a decline in value-added tax revenue – a key source of government income.
The strain is even greater for local authorities, as a prolonged slump in the property market has slashed a major revenue source – land sales – while they continue to provide most frontline public services.
Authorities have also stepped up scrutiny of overseas income, using big data to flag individuals who fail to report foreign earnings. In several cases last year, offenders were ordered to pay overdue taxes and penalties ranging from tens of thousands of yuan to millions of yuan.
China’s tax revenue increased by 1.8 per cent to 16.48 trillion yuan in the first 11 months of 2025, year on year, while personal income tax jumped 11.5 per cent to a record 1.47 trillion yuan, according to the Ministry of Finance.

data.title: How Chinese corruption busters are tracking down bribes paid in cryptocurrency
data.description:
Beijing has revealed how its graft busters are investigating bribes paid in hard-to-trace cryptocurrencies by tracking the ways in which corrupt officials have been spending the proceeds.
A documentary shown on state broadcaster CCTV on Wednesday highlighted the case of Yao Qian, the former director of the digital currency institute at the People’s Bank of China.
Investigators became suspicious when Yao bought a villa in Beijing for more than 20 million yuan (around US$2.9 million) under the name of a family member.
They later concluded that he had been given 2,000 ether coins by the director of an unnamed digital currency firm he had helped to list on an exchange and had cashed in 370 of these digital tokens for 10 million yuan in 2021 – when the currency’s value peaked.
Investigators from the Central Commission for Discipline Inspection (CCDI) also found that he had been given a cash bribe of 12 million yuan by an unnamed information services company.
Yao, 56, led the team that developed the digital renminbi and also headed technology supervision at China’s securities regulator, and the anti-corruption watchdog said he had used his influence within the crypto sector for his own gain.
Mainland China banned initial coin offerings in 2017, but a legal grey area meant that firms could continue to list on overseas exchanges.
Yao had tried to cover his tracks by using a “proxy account” to receive funds, investigators said, and had made four different blockchain transactions.
He was placed under investigation by the CCDI in April 2024 and expelled from the Communist Party in November that same year. He has also been convicted of undisclosed charges, but there is no official word about what sentence he has received.
According to the documentary, the investigation team attempted to reconstruct “the entire chain of transactions” for the virtual currency Yao had received.
They also seized three hardware wallets from Yao’s office that stored his cryptocurrency keys.
One of Yao’s subordinates, Jiang Guoqing, has also been placed under investigation. He told CCTV that he had helped Yao to evade scrutiny by providing a crypto wallet address to receive funds.
The CCTV documentary said that cryptocurrencies were “extremely difficult to regulate” because they existed as a “mere string of numbers online”.
But investigators told the programme that the details of blockchain transfers could be “accessed by anyone at any time” given the nature of blockchain and that any attempt to cash in these virtual currencies could be identified.
The investigation into Yao was a joint operation from the CCDI and disciplinary inspection personnel from Shanwei, a city in Guangdong province – a practice in line with China’s efforts to ensure that anti-corruption investigations are carried out impartially.
China was once the world’s largest cryptocurrency mining nation, but in September 2021, the authorities imposed a strict ban on virtual currency operations and banned Chinese citizens from trading on overseas exchanges.
Officials of Yao’s rank typically face strict restrictions on private overseas travel in an effort to prevent cross-border corruption and protect state secrets.
The documentary also featured the case of Li Yong, a former executive from the state-owned China National Offshore Oil Corporation who was jailed for 14 years for bribery last August.


The programme said 83 per cent of the bribes accepted by Li were from overseas, complicating the investigation, but added that Chinese law enforcement had worked with their international counterparts to obtain “crucial evidence” and establish “a complete chain of evidence both domestically and internationally”.
The episode also detailed how anti-corruption authorities in the eastern province of Zhejiang had been using big data and artificial intelligence to uncover corruption in the bidding process for municipal engineering projects.
Wednesday’s CCTV documentary coincided with the end of an annual CCDI meeting in Beijing, which called for “high-pressure deterrence” against corruption.
Its closing communique pledged to “intensify efforts to investigate cross-border corruption cases”.
